data.title: Earthquake of magnitude 6.2 strikes southeastern Afghanistan, GFZ says
data.description:
A magnitude 6.2 earthquake struck southeastern Afghanistan on Thursday, the German Research Centre for Geosciences said.

The quake was at a depth of 10 km (6.21 miles), GFZ said. The quake follows a series of earthquakes earlier this week that killed more than 2,200 people and injured over 3,600 in the country's east, flattening villages and leaving tens of thousands homeless.

The first earthquake of magnitude 6, one of Afghanistan's deadliest in recent years, unleashed widespread damage and destruction in the provinces of Kunar and Nangarhar on Sunday, when it struck at a shallow depth of 10 km (6 miles).

Survivors have been left homeless and aid groups warn of dwindling resources, with the United Nations and other agencies citing a critical need for food, shelter and medical supplies.

data.title: Western Digital not in a short-squeeze rally yet - S3
data.description:
Key points:
  • Wall Street indexes moderately higher
  • Consumer discretionary leads sector gains; utilities down most
  • Europe's STOXX index closes up ~0.6%
  • Dollar up; crude, gold, bitcoin down
  • U.S. 10-yr Treasury yields fall to ~4.18%

WESTERN DIGITAL NOT IN A SHORT-SQUEEZE RALLY YET- S3

Shares of Western Digital are up about 170% since their April lows, but the move for now does not seem to suggest a short squeeze, according to the S3 Partners research team.

Western Digital, which develops and manufactures data storage products, was up 4.8% on Thursday, putting the stock up about 100% for the year to date.

"Despite the rally, short interest has remained relatively flat—indicating this is not a short squeeze-driven move," they wrote in a note Thursday.

The steady rise has signaled by at least one measure - its "Short Squeeze Score" - possibly an elevated short squeeze risk, they said.

But there are reasons behind the rally, including Samsung's decision to cut chip production, strong investor sentiment around AI adoption and stronger earnings estimates, the team wrote. Momentum traders have also pushed the stock up.

At the same time, "implied volatility has risen in tandem with its Short Squeeze Score—suggesting options markets are pricing in potential turbulence."

"Investors need to be aware of squeeze risk potential, especially when the stock trades higher," the team wrote, adding that for now, it's not the case.

(Caroline Valetkevitch)

data.title: The Silent Heist: How Alzheimer's Bankrupts Families Long Before It Steals Memories & The $2 Million Fight Back
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / September 4, 2025 / The inheritance was not in a will. It was buried in bank statements, drained from 401(k)s, and etched in the exhausted eyes of a daughter who sold her future to buy her mother more time. This is the legacy Alzheimer's leaves in America; not just fading memory, but the quiet theft of financial security, dignity, and peace of mind, years before the final goodbye.

While headlines spotlight pharmaceutical breakthroughs, the reality in millions of homes is a different story: desperate arithmetic and impossible choices. Do I pay for my child's tuition or my parent's memory care? Do I work the night shift to pay the mortgage, or do I stay home to provide the 137 hours of unpaid care each month? Medicare provides the cruelest answer of all: $0 coverage for long-term custodial care.

In 2025, the Alzheimer's Family of Friends Foundation (AFOF) is refusing to let this crisis remain invisible. The nonprofit has launched a bold $2 million fundraising campaign; not to fund distant laboratory research, but to provide direct, immediate relief to caregivers and families who need help tonight.

The Math of Desperation

The crisis is staggering, and the numbers tell a grim story:

  • Skilled Nursing Facilities: More than $110,000 per year; the cost of a luxury estate for a single room.

  • Home Care: $21 per hour, or more than $40,000 annually for part-time assistance.

  • Unpaid Caregiving: 137 hours per month; the equivalent of a second full-time job.

"We've medicalized dementia while refusing to medicalize its care," says Dr. Eleanor Chang, geriatric economist. "It is like diagnosing cancer but forcing patients to buy their own chemotherapy pumps."

This vacuum has created what AFOF calls "the underground economy of desperation." Families are pawning heirlooms, selling military medals, and taking payday loans at 300% interest. Some even delay their own life-saving treatments to keep a parent alive.

Donate Now to stop this cycle of despair.

The $2 Million Lifeline: Where the Money Will Go

AFOF's campaign is a rebellion against financial collapse. Every dollar has a clear destination:

  • $1.5 Million: Direct Family Assistance - Emergency grants to stop evictions, subsidize adult day care, and cover what insurance ignores; from wheelchair pads to funeral costs.

  • $300,000: Administration & Oversight - Ensuring transparency, accountability, and efficient delivery of aid.

  • $200,000: Marketing & Outreach - Expanding awareness and reaching isolated families who believe they are alone.

This funding empowers AFOF's cornerstone programs: caregiver respite hours, crisis grants, financial navigation support, and dignity funds. These aren't abstract initiatives; they are lifelines.

Why This Matters Now

Alzheimer's research is essential, but science moves slowly. Families need relief today.

  • $3,500 can prevent a family from being evicted.

  • $18,000 funds a year of adult day care.

  • $110,000 pays for a full year in skilled nursing.

"Families are mortgaging their futures to provide care," says Miriam Okafor, Executive Director of AFOF. "We cannot ask them to wait decades for a cure. We must give them support before dinner tonight."

Be part of the resistance; Donate Now.

How You Can Help: Join the Family of Friends

AFOF offers multiple ways for individuals and corporations to take action:

  • The 24-Hour Challenge: All donations made today will be doubled by a coalition of matching donors.

  • The Continuous Care Circle: A pledge of $100/month covers exactly what Medicare won't; non-medical essentials that protect safety and dignity.

  • The Legacy Pledge: Designate AFOF in your estate plan, leaving future generations a legacy of compassion.

Ways to give:

Online: Donate now through AFOF's secure portal.

Corporate Sponsorships: Align your brand with a movement of dignity and compassion.

Mail Checks To:

Alzheimer's Family of Friends, Inc.

5999 Caladium Lane

Thomson, GA 30824

A Legacy Worth Leaving

"When you donate to AFOF, you're not just giving money; you're giving peace of mind," Okafor adds. "You are lifting the weight of impossible decisions off a caregiver's shoulders. You are giving a family their future back."

The inheritance of Alzheimer's doesn't have to be one of financial ruin. Together, we can rewrite the legacy into one of compassion, dignity, and shared responsibility.

Donate Now and become the lifeline a family desperately needs.

Press & Partnership Inquiries: press@theafof-foundation.org

Disclaimer:

This is a sponsored advocacy piece presented in partnership with the Alzheimer's Family of Friends Foundation (AFOF). The powerful narrative and urgent call to action reflect the editorial style and mission-focused perspective of the foundation and its supporters.

While the article is based on verified data from AFOF, AARP, and public health agencies, its primary aim is to illuminate the caregiver crisis and mobilize reader support. The emotional framing and descriptive language are intentionally used to convey the profound human and financial impact of Alzheimer's disease on families.

The statistics on care costs, caregiver hours, and charitable allocation of funds are presented as reported by the foundation. AFOF is a registered 501(c)(3) nonprofit organization, and all donations are tax-deductible to the fullest extent of the law.

Evrima Chicago maintains a strict distinction between its own fact-based reporting and sponsored content that supports community organizations. This piece falls into the latter category, published in recognition of the critical nature of the cause.

Readers are encouraged to learn more about AFOF's mission and financials directly at https://theafof-foundation.org.

data.title: Salesforce Outlook Leaves Questions About AI Growth — Market Talk
data.description: Salesforce's 2Q results are unlikely to change any views about the company, including concerns its AI investments may not be paying off enough, according to Oppenheimer. Third-quarter guidance "reveals a continuing tough macro environment," the analysts say, and "is unlikely to catalyze new opinions." The underwhelming sales outlook on the lighter end of expectations, is creating some restlessness among investors, Truist analyst Terry Tillman says. The 2Q report indicates that front-office AI is still in its very early stages, UBS analysts Karl Keirstead and Dean Marriott say, maintaining a neutral rating until there's a clearer path to more growth. Salesforce slides 5%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1315 ET - Salesforce's 2Q results are unlikely to change any views about the company, including concerns its AI investments may not be paying off enough, according to Oppenheimer. Third-quarter guidance "reveals a continuing tough macro environment," the analysts say, and "is unlikely to catalyze new opinions." The underwhelming sales outlook on the lighter end of expectations, is creating some restlessness among investors, Truist analyst Terry Tillman says. The 2Q report indicates that front-office AI is still in its very early stages, UBS analysts Karl Keirstead and Dean Marriott say, maintaining a neutral rating until there's a clearer path to more growth. Salesforce slides 5%. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1240 ET - Homebuilder D.R. Horton doesn't expect its margins to improve without a change in the housing environment, says Wedbush analyst Jay McCanless, citing a conversation with the company's management. The company sees opportunities to reduce labor costs while appreciation on land and development costs have slowed. But the incentives the company is offering to sell homes are still higher than historical levels, with affordability concerns from buyers and excess home inventory in some markets forcing builders to offer mortgage buydowns. "Netting it together, management does not anticipate gross margins can grow in the current environment without some help on the incentive front," McCanless says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1219 ET - Figma's outlook seems to have fallen short of high expectations among some on Wall Street, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a note. The company's guidance for revenue growth of about 37% over the fiscal year and 33% for the third quarter was higher than the analysts expected, but likely came in below lofty expectations from buyside analysts, they say. Figma's platform remains well positioned to address consolidating design workflows, but the pace at which it takes advantage of that opportunity will take time, given its recent launch of new products and plans to drive broad adoption of generative Ai tools before monetization, the analysts say. Shares fall 17% to $56.67. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1143 ET - Bird Construction is betting that infrastructure will be a key to buffing up earnings while Canada, and particularly Ontario, faces a construction slump. The Canadian construction company bought Fraser River Pile & Dredge, which opens up opportunities to capitalize on opportunities in infrastructure, including nation-building projects that support port expansion, transportation, trade, defence and energy requirements. The acquisition coincides with a the creation of the Major Projects Office by Prime Minister Carney to get nation-building projects built faster. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1118 ET - American Eagle's marketing campaigns with Sydney Sweeney and Travis Kelce generated 40 billion impressions and a record 700,000 new customers, but the company still has more work to do to improve its margins, Jefferies analysts say. Total revenue is still declining year-over-year and expenses are expected to surge in the third quarter due to elevated advertising spending. Meanwhile, tariffs are expected to cause a $60 million to $70 million hit to second-half margins. "While execution improved and brand heat is high, macro uncertainty, tariff drag, and flat FY comp guide (0%) suggest limited upside until visibility on margin recovery improves," the analysts say. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1111 ET - American Eagle's 2Q EPS beat isn't just attributable to its Sydney Sweeney ad buzz, UBS says. "While it is certainly true the marketing campaign has been a very big success (40B impressions), we believe the key to the stock is the inflection in AEO's Aerie business," the analysts say, adding that the Sweeney campaign was for the American Eagle brand and likely provided only tangential benefits to Aerie, which instead saw gains from improved products and merchandising. "If the market thinks Sydney Sweeney is the only reason AEO had a strong report, it will question the durability of AEO's momentum." American Eagle surges 28% to $17.49. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

1006 ET - Declining mortgage rates have pushed the median U.S. monthly housing payment down to $2,593, the lowest level since January, according to Redfin. The weekly average mortgage rate has ticked down to 6.56%, the lowest level in nearly a year. Lower monthly payments are leading to a modest uptick in home sales. Pending home sales rose 1.6% from a year earlier. But sales aren't surging, likely because it's still difficult for many Americans to afford a home. Home-sale prices rose 1.6% year over year during the four weeks ending Aug. 31, and while monthly payments have fallen from their peak, they're still up 5% from a year ago. "Mortgage rates haven't come down significantly enough to bring back a flood of buyers," Redfin says. Inventory has declined from its summer peak, one reason why sale prices are still rising. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

0955 ET - The exit of Duolingo's social media mastermind Zaria Parvez could make the company's marketing far more challenging, D.A. Davidson analyst Wyatt Swanson says. Parvez brought Duolingo from 50,000 to nearly 17 million followers on TikTok and fueled more than 8 billion-plus organic impressions across social media platforms, Swanson says. He believes Duolingo is not putting such a high focus on posting viral/edgy content, and thinks that when it does start putting in an effort to post again, the content may be met with negativity. "Without Zaria Parvez, it may be just that much more challenging to grow social once Duolingo starts aiming for virality again," Swanson says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0936 ET - Duolingo's active user growth continues to slow according to a D.A. Davidson proprietary dataset, says analyst Wyatt Swanson, who downgrades the stock to neutral from buy and lowers the price target to $300 from $500. The dataset shows active users have declined about 0.4% from mid-July to late August. That's a far cry from the average quarter-to-quarter increase (from 2Q to 3Q) of 7.2% daily active user growth over the past five years. "We expected a much more meaningful uptick in engagement following the return to school during August," Swanson says. The slowing user growth is primarily due to continued push-back from the company's AI-first strategy and minimal social media marketing. Duolingo falls 3% to $274 in early trading. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0851 ET - TD Cowen says that there's better momentum coming from Couche-Tard's U.S. same-store sales, and it should continue into F2Q. "While the consumer environment remains challenging, market share gains pushed U.S. same-store sales up about 1.5% in July and momentum built in August on the back of stronger execution in foodservice, beverages, and digital/loyalty strategies," analyst Michael Van Aelst says. This momentum should materialize into 2% growth of U.S. same-store sales in F2Q, he estimates.(adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0837 ET - Salesforce's top- and bottom-line beats in 2Q are being overshadowed by the company's underwhelming 3Q guidance, Wedbush analysts say in a research note. "We continue to believe that Salesforce can emerge as a long-term winner within the AI Revolution as the Agentforce strategy builds further incremental momentum and is still in the early innings of playing out, but we need to see improved execution moving forward from its core business while balancing aggressive investments into its data and AI strategies," the analysts write. Wedbush maintains its outperform rating and lowers its price target to $375 from $425. Salesforce falls 6% premarket. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

0758 ET - The business model of W.A.G. Payment Solutions, also know as Eurowag, looks strong, Peel Hunt analysts Gautam Pillai and Barun Singh write in a note. The U.K.-listed integrated payments and mobility platform delivered positive cash generation for the first half, with net debt falling and adjusted cash Ebitda growing 14.1% on year. The increase shows Eurowag's improving cash conversion and provides space for reinvestment and debt-reduction, they add. Shares are up 1.1% at 95.60 pence.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 1 PM ET: S&P 500, Dow Edge Higher | ADP ...
data.description:
S&P 500, Dow Edge Higher

Stocks posted moderate gains as President Trump's push for greater influence over the Federal Reserve faces a test.

----

ADP says just 54,000 private jobs created in August. Businesses still hesitant to hire.

Privately run businesses created just 54,000 jobs in August, ADP said, extending a string of poor employment reports that have raised alarms about the labor market and are likely to spur the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates soon.

----

A big part of the economy grew faster in August, ISM finds, but not because everything is A-OK

The large services side of the U.S. economy grew at the fastest pace in six months in August, but the improvement was driven by efforts to beat tariff-related price increases as businesses prepared for the Christmas shopping season.

----

Jobless claims rise to highest level since June, adding to concerns about labor market

What worries policymakers is that the labor market tends to weaken quickly once layoffs commence.

----

Trump Administration Seeks Swift Supreme Court Review on Tariffs

The Treasury secretary in a court submission said the lower-court loss is already hurting trade negotiations.

----

U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Post Unexpected Increase

U.S. crude oil inventories rose by 2.4 million barrels last week. Analysts had predicted stockpiles would fall by 2 million barrels.

----

U.K. Government's Debt Problems Might Not Be Worsened by Rise in Long-Term Yields

The U.K.'s government has struggled to halt a rise in its debt, but a recent increase in yields on its long-term bonds might not make its task much more difficult.

----

What to Look for in First Jobs Report Since Trump Fired BLS Chief

Economists and policymakers will be watching Friday's report closely to see if hiring continued to cool this summer.

----

Germany's Ifo Institute Cuts Growth Forecasts on Tariff Impact

The economy is set to grow by less than expected, as tariffs weigh and the government's planned fiscal loosening offers less of a boost to activity, leading economists said Thursday.

----

DOJ Opens Criminal Investigation Into Fed's Cook, Issues Subpoenas

The probe centers on whether the Fed governor submitted fraudulent information on mortgage applications.

----

Pump-and-Dump Worries Prompt Nasdaq to Tighten China Listing Rules

Some Wall Street veterans and investor-protection advocates have said such listings have become a hotbed for fraud and manipulation.

----

Swiss Inflation Holds Steady as SNB Mulls Rate Cut Below Zero

Inflation stayed low in August, keeping the country's central bank on guard as it considers cutting its key interest rate to below zero later this month.

----

Chinese Stocks Pull Back After Red-Hot Rally

Equities swooned in both China and Hong Kong, led by semiconductor and tech hardware stocks, which had risen the most over the past weeks.

data.title: DPO Launches Greenlight Data Centers with New Investor Funding to Accelerate AI Data Center Development
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / September 4, 2025 / Digital Power Optimization, Inc. ("DPO"), a developer at the intersection of high-density computing and energy infrastructure, today announced the launch of Greenlight Data Centers ("Greenlight"), a new platform focused on accelerating the development of AI-ready data center sites. While DPO continues its work with proof-of-work computing and renewable energy integration, Greenlight will operate independently to deliver energized, entitled, and strategically located AI-ready data center infrastructure where firm power is available and AI demand is growing.

Greenlight was created to address a fundamental breakdown in the infrastructure market: while AI demand has surged, energized land and build-ready sites have lagged far behind. Greenlight solves this disconnect with a power-first model, securing locations that already offer 20 to 100 megawatts (MW) of firm capacity, robust fiber interconnection points, and minimal permitting hurdles, turning underutilized substations and latent energy into operational assets.

"Greenlight Data Centers delivers the rackspace AI builders urgently need, without the delays that typically plague large-scale data center development," said Alex Stoewer, CEO of Greenlight Data Centers. "By focusing on AI-ready data centers that are 20 to 100 MW in size, we leverage existing electrical infrastructure in regions with excess capacity, places where speed is possible, and energy is available now. We're not speculating-we're executing."

Drawing on years of experience deploying computing infrastructure with utilities and IPPs, the Greenlight team, led by Stoewer, brings a track record of building at the edge of innovation. As co-founders of DPO, Greenlight's leadership has previously deployed data centers across the U.S., directly connected to wind, solar, hydro, and gas generation assets. This work has created trusted partnerships with power providers and local co-ops, setting the stage for this next chapter focused on AI.

Greenlight's sites are located in markets with high concentrations of renewable power and where high-density compute can be deployed quickly. The company's development process, branded as NextSight, takes projects from site control to notice-to-proceed in months, not years. The model is simple: deliver energized, entitled, and AI-optimized infrastructure faster than anyone else.

"We've been building data centers in overlooked places with excess power since 2020," said Andrew Webber, CEO of DPO and Chairman of Greenlight Data Centers. "Hyperscalers have snapped up nearly every available large utility footprint on the grid. Opportunities are available, but they are fragmented and located outside of major hubs, and we know how to find, evaluate, and act on those opportunities better than anyone else."

Greenlight Data Centers is actively developing 20 to 100 MW projects across the central U.S. The company expects to bring its first energized sites online in 2027.

About Greenlight Data Centers

Greenlight Data Centers develops power-first, AI-ready data center infrastructure fast. Focused on sites with existing utility infrastructure and strong fiber connectivity, Greenlight delivers entitled, ready-to-build projects with compressed timelines and strong returns on capital. For more information, visit greenlightdc.com

About Digital Power Optimization, Inc.

Digital Power Optimization is a leading developer and operator of green data centers for power-dense computing. DPO partners with utilities and IPPs to locate data center facilities on-site at power generation plants to directly utilize renewable energy for the operation of HPC/AI and Proof-of-Work computing. DPO is privately held. For more information, visit https://www.digitalpoweroptimization.com/

data.title: Goldman’s new retail gambit is all about control
data.description:
The bank will invest up to $1 bln in T. Rowe Price, in exchange stuffing its in-house private equity or credit into the asset manager’s funds. It’s a twist on a wave of such tie-ups that puts CEO David Solomon in the driver’s seat to redeem his uneven push for the masses.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Stephen Gandel on LinkedIn and X.

CONTEXT NEWS

Goldman Sachs and T. Rowe Price announced a partnership on September 4. The two firms plan to create target date funds and other investment products aimed at retirement accounts that blend public and private assets. As part of the deal, Goldman Sachs is investing directly in T. Rowe Price, spending up to $1 billion to buy a stake in the asset manager through open-market purchases.

data.title: Ciena Will Lean Into AI Investments, Cut Up to 5% of Workforce — Update
data.description:
(17:05 GMT) *Ciena CEO Notes a 'Strategic Inflection Point' in AI >CIEN
(17:05 GMT) *Ciena Is Benefiting From Accelerating Demand, CEO Says >CIEN
(17:05 GMT) *Ciena Gave FY26 Outlook Early Due to 'Massive Backlog,' CEO Says >CIEN
(17:05 GMT) *Ciena Will Continue Investing in Supply Chains, Scaling Operations, CEO Says >CIEN
(17:06 GMT) Ciena Will Lean Into AI Investments, Cut Up to 5% of Workforce — Update

By Connor Hart

Ciena will cut up to 5% of its workforce, or roughly 430 jobs, as it shifts investment from slower-growth areas like residential broadband to next-generation technologies that are driving demand.

Chief Executive Gary Smith said the networking-systems and software company has recently benefited from a strategic inflection point in artificial intelligence, as hyperscalers turn their focus from computing power to the networks needed to enable the technology.

"No one wants to strand all the investments they've made in large language models, data centers, and graphics processing units because they didn't invest in the connectivity of the network," he said Thursday. "That obviously plays to our strength in high-speed connectivity."

Accelerating demand for Ciena's networking platforms boosted both its top and bottom lines in the recent quarter. Shares surged 23% to $116.44, hitting a more than 20-year high, after the company said sustained demand is expected to drive another year of revenue growth.

"We're so confident in the durability of this that we gave guidance a quarter early," Smith said, in part citing the company's "massive backlog." The midpoint of the company's fiscal 2026 revenue forecast implies roughly 17% growth year over year — similar to the increase expected this year.

To meet demand, Ciena has been expanding its supply chain and scaling operations to ensure it can meet the needs of hyperscalers, such as Amazon and Microsoft, as well as emerging neoscalers, or more specialized cloud providers like OpenAI.

Neoscalers are "a whole new market for us," Smith said, "and they are leaning into the network straight away."

In the current quarter, Ciena expects to record roughly $20 million in restructuring charges related to its layoffs. Over the longer term, Smith said the savings will be redirected toward the company's AI workloads, allowing for increased investment in high-return businesses while keeping operating expenses largely flat over the next year.

Ciena had 8,657 employees as of Nov. 2, according to the latest count available in filings with the Securities and Exchange Commission.

The company reported profit of $50.3 million, or 35 cents a share, for its fiscal third quarter ended Aug. 2, up from $14.2 million, or 10 cents a share, a year earlier. Adjusted earnings per share were 67 cents, ahead of the 53 cents that analysts polled by FactSet expected.

Revenue jumped 29% to $1.22 billion, topping Wall Street's $1.17 billion forecast. Networking platforms, which account for more than three-fourths of sales, notched sales of $941.4 million, up 35% from a year earlier, while revenue from the company's global-services unit climbed 20% to $160.2 million.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

data.title: Gold Breakout: $3,606.6 Shatters Previous Ceiling
data.description:
Gold has reached it's new all-time high at $3606.6 today as of 12:02 PM, Thursday, September 4th, 2025Eastern Daylight Time (EDT) zone.

Following a four-month of consolidation moves since April 2025, gold (XAUUSD) staged a breakoutsurging from $3,316 per ounce at the August 20 to the High price of $3,606.6 per ounce by September 4, a new all-time high for gold!

And this rally involves a multi-factor surge that pushed prices to beat the previous April peak at $3,500 per ounce.

Interestingly, analysts believe the momentum isn't fading just yet. Here's what lit the fuse:

Key Drivers Behind the Rally

  • U.S. Dollar Weakness

The dollar slipped over the past month, making gold cheaper for non-dollar investors and boosting demand.

  • Geopolitical Tensions

Ongoing global uncertaintyespecially around trade and U.S. policydrove investors toward gold as a safe haven.

  • Central Bank Buying

The World Gold Council reported that central banks accumulated a total of 10 tonnes of gold in August, led by Kazahstan with 3 tonnespurchase. Turkey, Czech Republic and China each added 2 tonnes. Notably, China has now logged 9 consecutive months of reported buying. Although the pas has slowed, this continued accumulation still contributes to upward pressure on gold prices.

  • ETF Inflows

Gold ETFs saw $5.5 billion in inflows in August alone, with North America leading the charge.

  • Rate Cut Expectations

Markets are pricing in a possible Fed rate cut in September, which lowers the opportunity cost of holding gold (since gold doesnt yield interest).

Bonus Insight: Seasonal Momentum

Source: Forbes

Cycles Research Investments LLC has shared its findings indicating that the period between June and September has historically been the most bullish for gold.

And this year is no exception, technical analysis suggests a potential rally towad $3,700 per ounce-a target that appears increasingly plausible, especially considering that as of today early September, gold has already at $3606.6.

Disclaimer: While this analysis outlines key technical indicators and potential price targets, it should not be interpreted as a definitive buy signal. Market conditions are fluid, and individual risk tolerance, portfolio strategy, and macroeconomic factors must be considered. Readers are strongly encouraged to conduct further analysis or consult a financial advisor before making any investment decisions.
